ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Murgh Kundan Quliya. FOODON_03315595 (chicken curry) is the closest parent category covering braised spiced chicken dishes.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (chicken meat dish) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Murgh Kundan Quliya or dum-braised Mughal chicken preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific Mughal chicken preparation. SNOMED CT does not cover regional Indian dish variants at this level of specificity.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf, black pepper)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Murgh Kundan Quliya is approximately 300 g, comprising braised chicken pieces in a rich spiced gravy, served as a main course.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Murgh Kundan Quliya is a Mughal dum-braised chicken preparation. No exact entry exists in ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry (cooked, bone-in) as the closest approved regional analogue. The dish uses yogurt, ghee, and whole spices consistent with the ICMR-NIN chicken curry profile. Minor upward adjustment to fat is applied to reflect ghee-based cooking typical of Mughal preparations. All values are per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Murgh Kundan Quliya. GI is estimated at 35 based on analogous low-carbohydrate braised meat curry dishes reported in Atkinson et al. (2008). The dish is predominantly protein and fat with minimal carbohydrate (3.2 g/100 g), consistent with low-GI meat-based curry preparations. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 3.2 × 3) / 100 = 3.36, rounded to 3.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1260 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute approximately 50–60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~35)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Murgh Kundan Quliya has a low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate load, making it a suitable protein-rich main course for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee and yogurt (~3.8 g/100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size due to saturated fat from ghee-based cooking; choosing lean chicken pieces and requesting reduced ghee preparation can lower saturated fat intake to within heart-healthy guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murgh Kundan Quliya is a classic Mughal-era North Indian braised chicken dish prepared using the dum cooking technique, in which marinated chicken morsels are slow-cooked in a sealed vessel over low heat with aromatic whole spices including green cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaves, and black pepper. The name derives from Urdu: 'murgh' (chicken), 'kundan' (pure gold, referring to the golden hue of the saffron-enriched gravy), and 'quliya' (a style of braised meat preparation in Mughal court cuisine). The dish features a rich, mildly spiced, yogurt-based gravy with a delicate saffron aroma and a tender, fall-off-the-bone chicken texture. It is distinct from heavier tomato-based curries, relying instead on the natural juices of the chicken, yogurt, and whole spices for its flavour profile. Nutritionally, Murgh Kundan Quliya is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-glycaemic dietary patterns. The fat content is primarily from ghee and yogurt, contributing saturated fat that warrants moderation for cardiovascular health. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on preparation. The dish is naturally gluten-free and is typically halal. In Singapore, it is served at specialist Indian restaurants such as Dumpukht Grills & Curry, representing the Mughal culinary heritage within the South Asian diaspora food scene.
common_user_queries: What does Kundan Quliya taste like?
Is Murgh Kundan Quliya gluten free?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Murgh Kundan Quliya exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry (cooked) as the closest approved regional analogue. GI is estimated from analogous low-carbohydrate braised meat curry data in Atkinson et al. (2008). Confidence is medium rather than low because the dish composition is well-documented in culinary literature and the ICMR-NIN analogue is a strong match for the macronutrient profile.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN chicken curry (cooked, bone-in) as no exact entry exists for Murgh Kundan Quliya in any approved primary source. A minor upward adjustment to fat_g reflects ghee-based Mughal preparation style. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN chicken curry source entry. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN chicken curry source entry. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN chicken curry source entry. 5. GI is estimated at 35 based on analogous braised meat curry data from Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 6. The dish is served at Indian House / Dumpukht Grills & Curry, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey data source). 7. Chinese names are transliterations as no established Chinese culinary name exists for this Mughal dish. 8. Malay alias reflects the dish name as used in Singapore's Indian restaurant context. 9. is_halal is set to true as the dish uses chicken and no pork or alcohol ingredients; however, halal certification of the specific restaurant has not been independently verified.
